steps.title: Respect the audience
steps.content: The audience already knows the Atlantic Monthly, so the sentence should introduce Rebecca Alden's book rather than explain the magazine's background. The useful notes are the book's title and its subject: female authors whose autobiographies appeared in the magazine in the early 1900s.

steps.title: Reject magazine-centered options
steps.content:
• Choice B: It introduces the Atlantic Monthly as a magazine and gives its founding date, which is background the audience does not need.
  • Choice C: It also supplies background about when the magazine began and what it focused on instead of concentrating on Alden's book.
  • Choice D: It mentions Juanita Harrison, one author discussed by Alden, but that example is too narrow to introduce the book as a whole.

steps.title: Choose the book focus
steps.content: The best sentence names Alden's book and identifies its overall subject without treating the magazine itself as new information. That matches the goal of introducing the book to readers who are already familiar with the Atlantic Monthly. The answer is A.
